Facts.text: Visigothic King Sisenand convenes Fourth Council of Toledo at the church of Saint Leocadia in Toledo; all bishops of Hispania agree to establish seminaries in cathedral cities and implement educational standards
Ramon Berenguer II, Count of Barcelona is assassinated
Pope Boniface VIII's decree Ausculta fili (only nominee)
The French Franc is created to pay the ransom of King John II of France, captured by the English during the Hundred Years War
Emir Edigu of Golden Horde reaches Moscow
Earthquake strikes Naples, killing an estimated 35,000 people
Jews are expelled from Portugal by order of King Manuel I
Italian bishop Niccolo Sfondrati chosen as Pope Gregory XIV
Giulio Caccini and Ottario Rinuccini's opera "Euridice" premieres at the Pitti Palace, in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany
Russian princess Elisabeth Petrovna seizes power from the infant Ivan VI, with the help of the military
Revolt in Genoa against Spanish rule
Battle of Leuthen: Prussian army beats an Austrian force
World's largest auction house - Christie's hold their first sale in their permanent saleroom in Pall Mall, London
First US academic honor society, Phi Beta Kappa, forms at William & Mary College in Williamsburg, Virginia
Dutch troops occupy Hasselt
Lübeck surrenders to allied armies
Foundation of Maceió in Brazil
Former US President John Q Adams takes his seat as a member of House of Representatives
Assault on Mexican-held San Antonio by Texan rebels
Hector Berlioz's "Requiem" premiere at Les Invalides, conducted by François Habeneck
Uprising under William Lyon Mackenzie in Canada
German-Swiss chemist Christian Freidrich Schoenbein obtains a patent for cellulose nitrate explosive
Aaron Allen of Boston patents folding theater chair
Chincha Islands War: Peru allies with Chile against Spain.
1st American bicycle college opens (NY)
The ship Mary Celeste is discovered mysteriously abandoned by her crew in the Atlantic Ocean
Daniel Stillson of Massachusetts patents 1st practical pipe wrench
Fire at Brooklyn Theater kills 295, trampled or burned to death
1st automatic telephone switching system patented

US Patent Office grants patent #268503, for the exclusive right to make, use or sell "animal-shaped buildings"  to James V. Lafferty of Philadelphia; Three novelty elephants are built: Elephant Bazaar in South Atlantic City, New Jersey  (1881); Light of Asia in South Cape may New Jersey (1884; and Elephantine Colossus in Coney Island, New York (1885)
Stanley's expedition reaches plateau at Lake Albert Congo
Hector Berlioz's opera "Les Troyens" premieres in Karlsruhe, Germany
Anti-semite Hermann Ahlwardt elected to Germany's Reichstag
Electric car built at the Dixon Carriage works in Toronto, could go 15 miles between charges
Georges Feydeaus' comedic play "L'hôtel du libre échange" premieres in Paris
Henry Campbell-Bannermam (Lib) becomes UK Prime Minister
1st US football uniform numerals used (University of Pittsburgh)
The Triple Alliance among Italy, Austria, and Germany (originally signed in 1882) is renewed for six years, beginning in 1914, a move inspired by instability in the Balkans
The Italian Parliament proclaims the neutrality of the country
Austro-German Forces launch an offensive against the Italians on the western end of their line, around Asiago
Oil refinery on Curacao opens
Dimitrios Rallis forms a government in Greece.
Pro football playoff game Akron & Buffalo 0-0 tie, title undecided
Hamilton Tiger Red Green scores 5 goals to beat Tor Maple Leafs 10-5
England defeats Australia by record 675 runs at Brisbane
Wilhelm Miklas elected president of Austria
1st US nudist organization, the American League for Physical Culture in New York City
Prohibition ends in the US at 5:32 PM EST when the 21st Amendment to the US Constitution is ratified, repealing the 18th Amendment
First commercial hydroponics operation established (Montebello, California)
National Council of Negro Women forms by Mary McLeod Bethune (NYC)
Armenian SSR, Azerbaijan SSR, Georgian SSR, Kazakh SSR & Kirghiz SSR becomes constituent republics of Soviet Union
"Sullivan's Travels", directed by Preston Sturges and starring Joel McCrea and Veronica Lake, is released
Football Writers Association of America organized
Patrick Hamilton's "Angel Street" premieres in NYC
Sister Elizabeth Kenny new treatment for infantile paralysis approved
Soviet anti-offensive in Moscow drives out Nazi army
Seyss-Inquart orders students in nazi-Germany to work
West Indies chocolate/coffee drop above Netherland
German troops steal all the silver coin in Utrecht
Dutch Special Council of Annulment affirms death sentence of radio presenter Max Blokzijl for broadcasting Nazi propaganda
Flight 19, the "Lost Squadron" of five torpedo bombers and 14 airmen, is lost east of Florida in the supposed Bermuda Triangle
NY Giant Charley Conerly sets NFL record of 36 pass completions
Sikkim becomes a protectorate of India
Great Smog of London (England): Killer fog creeps in, as still winds and cold temperatures cause pollution fueled by coal smoke to stagnate; lasting 4 days, over 8,000 deaths attributed to conditions
KTEW (now KJRH) TV channel 2 in Tulsa, OK (NBC) begins broadcasting
American Federation of Labor (AFL) and the Congress of Industrial Organizations (CIO) merge to form the AFL-CIO, with George Meany as President
17-year-old British swimmer Judy Grinham and American Carin Cone both swim world record 1:12.9 in the women's 100m backstroke at the Melbourne Olympics; Grinham is awarded gold medal
Danish yachtsman Paul Elvstrøm wins the Finn class gold medal at the Melbourne Olympics; his 2nd straight Finn gold and 3rd consecutive Olympic gold in sailing
NYC is first US city to legislate against racial or religious discrimination in housing market (Fair Housing Practices Law)
William Inge's "Dark at the Top of the Stairs" premieres in NYC
Phils drop plans for NY sportcast as Yanks threat to do same in Philadelphia
The Preston bypass, the UK's first stretch of motorway, opens to traffic for the first time. It is now part of the M6 and M55 motorways.
WTOL TV channel 11 in Toledo, OH (CBS) begins broadcasting
Future Pakistan cricket captain Intikhab Alam bowls Australian opener Colin McDonald with his first ball in Test cricket in drawn 3rd Test in Karachi
Ghana drops diplomatic relations with Belgium
Captain Roger Donlon is awarded the first Medal of Honor of the Vietnam War for successfully repelling a large Viet Cong attack
Brunswick Records releases "My Generation", the debut album of British rock group The Who, in the UK and Australia
Four-node ARPANET network is established between the University of California, Los Angeles, Stanford Research Institute, U.C. Santa Barbara, and the University of Utah
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
NFL Los Angeles Rams running back Willie Ellison sets NFL record of 247 yards rushing
Premiere of Dario Fo's Morte accidentale di un anarchico
Stanley Cup, Conn Smythe Trophy & Bill Masterson Trophy stolen from Hockey Hall of Fame
KCBJ (now KMIZ) TV channel 17 in Columbia, MO (ABC) 1st broadcast
Libya nationalizes British Petroleum concession
38th Heisman Trophy Award: Johnny Rodgers, Nebraska (FL)
Australia Labour party wins parliamentary election
Joseph A Walker's "River Niger" premieres in NYC
Chicago Cubs Ron Santo becomes 1st baseball player to invoke 10-5 rule and veto his trade (with the California Angels)
1st World Football League Bowl, Birmingham Americans beat Florida
Airport terminal roof in Tehran collapses killing 17
NFL's Seattle Seahawks forms
Oliver Tilden Triangle in the Bronx named
NASA launches space vehicle S-196, it failed
Egypt breaks off diplomatic relations with Syria, Libya, Algeria, Iraq & South Yemen
New York Islanders took 28 shots in 1 period vs Pittsburgh Penguins; Penguins' Ross Lonsberry failed on 7th penalty shot against Islanders
Pioneer Venus 1 begins orbiting Venus
Sam Shepard's "Buried Child" premieres in NYC
Bank of Canada's Canadian Currency Museum opens
47th Heisman Trophy Award: Marcus Allen, Southern Cal (RB)
France performs nuclear test
Dutch judoka Ingrid Berghmans retains judo's world championship
Mel Gray ends NFL streak of 121 consecutive game receptions
NFL Cleveland Browns' Brian Sipe sets club record with 33 pass completions
Seattle Univ Baptist Ch declares sanctuary for Cen Am refugees
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
12 killed by a car bomb shattering 9-story building in west Beirut
ICIMOD established and inaugurated with its headquarters in Kathmandu, Nepal, and legitimised through an Act of Parliament in Nepal in the same year.
LA Dodger pitcher Steve Howe is suspended for 1 year for cocaine use
French colonies killed 10 Kanaken in New Caledonia
New York Yankees trade catcher Rick Cerone to Atlanta Braves for pitcher Brian Fisher
Dow Jones Industrial Average rose above 1,500 level for 1st time
Great Britain performs nuclear test
Sam Shepard's "Lie of the Mind" premieres in NYC
53rd Heisman Trophy Award: Tim Brown, Notre Dame (WR)
Schonbrunn skates world record 3 km ladies (4:16.76)
Shuttle Atlantis launches world's 1st nuclear-war-fighting satellite
France TGV train reaches world record speed of 482.4 kph
Blue Jays trade F McGriff & T Fernandez to SD for R Alomar & J Carter
Former Noriega aide Luis del Cid pleads guilty
Iraq announces willingness to speak with US about resolving the Persian Gulf crisis
Charles Keating Jr (Lincoln Savings & Loan fraud), found guilty
NY Daily News files for protection under chapter 11
Astronauts begin repair of Hubble telescope in space
Mayor of Vienna Helmut Zilk is injured by a letter bomb
Rafael Caldera elected President of Venezuela
Sri Lankan government announces the conquest of Tamil Tigers' Jaffna stronghold
"Dreams & Nightmares" opens at Martin Beck Theater NYC
Players union approves new collective bargaining agreement
Portland's Jermaine O'Neal at 18 becomes the youngest NBA player
First game at Washington Capitals' MCI Center
Space Shuttle mission STS 87 (Columbia 24) lands back on Earth
"O Brother, Where Art Thou" soundtrack to the film of the same name released
Civil Partnership Act comes into effect in the United Kingdom, allowing the country's first civil partnerships to be registered
Lake Tanganyika earthquake causes significant damage, mostly in the Democratic Republic of the Congo
Westroads Mall massacre: A gunman opens fire with a semi-automatic rifle at an Omaha, Nebraska mall, killing eight people before taking his own life.
8 people are killed and 12 injured by a magnitude 5.6 earthquake in South Korea
In his Autumn Statement, Chancellor of the Exchequer George Osborne cuts the UK growth forecast for 2013 to 1.2% from the 2% forecast in the budget
52 people are killed and 167 are injured in a militant attack on a defense ministry compound in Sana'a, Yemen
Malta becomes the 1st country in Europe to outlaw conversion therapy
Artist Lubaina Himid wins Britain's Turner Prize, oldest person at 63 and first nonwhite woman
Austrian courts rule in favour of same-sex marriage from 2019
Democratic representative John Conyers resigns from US Congress after allegations of sexual harassment
Netflix fires actor Danny Masterson from "The Ranch" after allegations of rape filed against him by 3 women
Online store Amazon begins operations in Australia
Russia is banned from the next Winter Olympics in South Korea for state-sponsored doping
Arrest of Huawei Financial Officer Meng Wanzhou for possible violation of Iran sanctions in Canada
CO2 emissions reach all-time high (up 2.7%) driven by coal use in China and consumption of oil, in Global Carbon Project report
Letter byAlbert Einsteinfrom 1954 on concept of religion sells for $2.9 million at Christie's in New York
US state funeral for former presidentGeorge H. W. Bush, eulogy by George W. Bush, attended by President Donald Trump predecessors Barack Obama, Bill Clinton and Jimmy Carter
Wisconsin Republican senate passes bills restricting power of new Democratic governor Tony Evers
National strike in France; more than 800,000 people in 100 cities protest against proposed pension reform
Saudi state-owned oil company Saudi Aramco raises a record $25.6 billion in its first public share offering, makes it the world's most valuable company at $1.7 trillion
WHO says 142,000 people died of the measles around the world in 2018, nearly 20,000 more than in 2017
Russia begins vaccinating people with its Sputnik V vaccine in Moscow, despite the vaccine not finishing clinical trials
First known Hippos tests positive for COVID-19 at Antwerp Zoo in Belgium, with staff putting Imani (14) and Hermien (41) into isolation

Construction begins on world's biggest radio telescope - the Square Kilometre Array (SKA) in South Africa and Australia with collection area of nearly 500,000 square meters. Built to test Einstein's theories and search for extraterrestrial life
Fifteenth meeting of the Conference of the Parties to the Convention on Biological Diversity (COP15) opens in Montreal, Canada
Iranian senior official confirms the country's Morality Police has been shut down in a concession to three months of nationwide protests, though may take on a different form
Sculptor Jesse Darling wins the Turner Prize for his sculptures made of everyday objects
Mathematicians solve a 122 year-old puzzle known as “Dudeney’s dissection” - how to dissect an equilateral triangle into a square, using the smallest amount of pieces
Roman consulCicerodelivers the last of his famous Catiline Orations before the Senate on the fate of the Cataline conspirators
Charlemagnebecomes the sole King of the Franks after the death of his brother Carloman
Christopher Columbusis the first European to sight Hispaniola (modern Dominican Republic and Haiti)
First copies purchased ofWilliam Shakespeare's First Folio, collecting his plays posthumously into one volume, bought by Edward Dering

English pirateBlackbeardransacks the merchant sloop "Margaret" and keeps her captain, Henry Bostock, prisoner for 8 hours before releasing him. Bostock later provides the first record of Blackbeard's appearance and the source for his name
George Washingtonre-elected as US President
Thomas Jeffersonre-elected US President, andGeorge Clintonelected Vice-President
Hector Berlioz's program symphony "Symphonie fantastique" premieres at the Paris Conservatoire
Andrew Jacksonre-elected US President after defeatingHenry Clay
Jefferson Davisis appointed to fill vacant US Senate seat for Mississippi
Dion Boucicault's stage melodrama "The Octaroon" opens at The Winter Garden Theatre, NYC
Battle of Coffeeville: Confederate forces haltUlysses S. Grant's Mississippi invasion via Tennessee
John Thompsonbecomes the 4th Prime Minister of Canada.
Henrik Ibsen's play "Kejsor og Galileer" (Emperor and Galilean) premieres in Leipzig
Hans Lutherforced to resign as Chancellor and head of the German Weimar government
German physicistAlbert Einsteingranted a visa to enter America
US PresidentHarry Trumancreates Committee on Civil Rights by Exec Order #9808
Joe LouisbeatsJersey Joe Walcottin 15 for heavyweight boxing title, at Madison Square Garden, NYC
Ezzard CharlesKOs Nick Barone in 11 for heavyweight boxing title
Historic bus boycott begins in Montgomery, Alabama byRosa Parksand other civil rights activists
Montgomery Improvement Association formed byRalph Abernathy,Martin Luther King Jr.and Edgar Nixon to support the Montgomery bus protest in Montgomery, Alabama
Thornton Wilder's play "Matchmaker" premieres on Broadway, NYC

Soviet artistic gymnastLarisa Latyninawins the women's vault and ties for gold in the floor exercise section at the Melbourne Olympics; later takes gold total to 4 with teams and individual all-round titles
President Sukarnoof Indonesia expels all Dutch people.
Subscriber Trunk Dialling (STD) is inaugurated in the UK byQueen Elizabeth IIwhen she speaks to the Lord Provost in a call from Bristol to Edinburgh.
PediatricianBenjamin Spockand poetAllen Ginsbergarrested in New York while protesting against the Vietnam War
Future Hockey Hall of Fame centerPhil Espositoof the Boston Bruins scores 2 goals in his goaltender brother Tony's (also HOF) NHL debut for the Montreal Canadiens; 2-2 tie

Apple Records releases the single "Come and Get It" by the band Badfinger; track was written and produced byPaul McCartneyfor the dark comedy film "The Magic Christian" starringPeter SellersandRingo Starr
Ian Duryperforms his first gig with Kilburn and the High Roads at Croydon School of Art
Apple Records releasesPaul McCartney& Wings album "Band on the Run" in US; the commercial and critical pinnacle of his post-Beatle work tops the charts in 7 countries
Final episode ofMonty Python's Flying Circus airs on BBC TV
Baseball free agentPete Rosesigns 4-year, $32 million contract with Philadelphia Phillies becoming highest paid baseball player
Ireland premierJack Lynchresigns
Oakland Athletics trade outfielderRickey Hendersonand pitcher Bert Bradley to the New York Yankees for Tim Birtsas, Jay Howell, Stan Javier, Eric Plunk, and José Rijo.
Boris Beckerbeats defending championIvan Lendl5–7, 7–6, 3–6, 6–2, 7–6 in a classic final to win his first of 3 ATP Masters Grand Prix tennis titles at Madison Square Garden, NYC
Federal grand jury indicts The PTL Club founder and Christian evangelistJim Bakkerfor fraud after he paid hush money to cover up an alleged rape
British Indian novelistSalman Rushdieappears in public for 1st time in 2 years after Iran called for his assassination
"Ocean's Eleven" directed bySteven Soderbergh, starringGeorge Clooney,Brad Pitt,Matt DamonandJulia Robertspremieres in Westwood, California
"The Two Towers" 2ndLord of the Ringsfilm directed byPeter Jacksonand starringElijah WoodandIan McKellenpremieres in New York
Mexican singer Thalia the "Queen of Latin Pop" is awarded a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame in Los Angeles
Audrey Hepburn'sGivenchylittle black dress from film "Breakfast at Tiffany's" auctioned for charity for record £467,200 ($923,187) at Christie's, London
CommodoreFrank Bainimaramaoverthrows the government in Fiji
Greg Madduxsigns a one-year deal with the San Diego Padres worth $10 million
Former NFL starO.J. Simpsonis sentenced to 33 years in prison for kidnapping and armed robbery
Human remains previously found in 1991 are finally identified by Russian and American scientists as those of TsarNicholas II
Twitter reveals K-pop boy bandBTSwere the most tweeted-about celebrities in 2017
National day of mourning for former US PresidentGeorge H. W. Bushas he lies in state at the Capitol Rotunda, Washington
Speaker of the US House of Representatives,Nancy Pelosi, announces articles of impeachment against US PresidentDonald Trumpwill be drawn up for abuse of power

Kennedy Center Honors ceremony held for singer-songwriterJoni Mitchell, singer-actressBette Midler, record mogulBerry Gordy, producerLorne Michaelsand opera singer Justino Diaz, with President Biden attending,in Washington D.C.
500 Jews of the Free Imperial City of Nuremberg are massacred during Black Death riots, part of a wave of pogroms across Western Europe
US PresidentJames K. Polktriggers the Gold Rush of 1849 by confirming a gold discovery in California
US aircraft carrier Lexington and 5 heavy cruisers leave Pearl Harbor
Jianwen, 2nd Emperor of the Ming Empire (1398-1402), born in China (d. 1402)
Nicolas Cleynaerts, Flemish grammarian, born in Diest, Belgium (d. 1542)
Ashikaga Yoshiaki, 15th and last Japanese shōgun of the Ashikaga shogunate (1568-73), born in Japan (d. 1597)
Fausto Paolo Sozzini, Italian theologian and founder of Socinianism, born in Siena, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1604)
Ubbo Emmius [van Embden], Dutch-Frisian humanist and historian, born in Greetsiel, East Frisia (d. 1625)
Robert Haley, 1st Earl of Oxford and English premier (Whig, 1710-14), born in Covent Garden, Middlesex, Kingdom of England (d. 1725)

Robert Harley, English diplomat and statesman (Lord High Treasurer, 1711-14; Chancellor of the Exchequer, 1710-11; Secretary of State, 1704-08), born in Covent Garden, Middlesex, Kingdom of England (d. 1724)
Francesco Scarlatti, Italian composer, born in Palermo, Sicily (d. ~1741)
Francesco Geminiani, Italian violinist and composer, born in Lucca, Republic of Lucca (d. 1762)
Giuseppe de Majo, Italian composer and organist, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples, Spanish Empire (d. 1771)
Joseph Friebert, Austrian composer, born in Gnadendorf, Lower Austria (d. 1799)
Henry Drummond, English banker and religious leader, born in The Grange, Hampshire (d. 1860)
Fyodor Tyutchev, Russian poet and statesman, born in Bryansk, Oryol Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1873)
Lorenzo Langstroth, American apiarist (invented modern beehive), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1895)[1]
Afanasy Fet, Russian poet, born in Mtsensk, Russia (d. 1892)
Elizabeth Cabot Agassiz, American educator and the co-founder and first president of Radcliffe College, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1907)
John Benjamin Sanborn, American lawyer, politician, and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Epsom, New Hampshire (d. 1904)
Henri-Gustave Joly de Lotbinière, French-Canadian politician (7th Lieutenant Governor of British Columbia), born in Épernay, France (d. 1908)
Christina Rossetti, English poet (Winter Rain, Passing Away), born in London, England (d. 1894)
Marcus Daly, Irish-American mining tycoon, born in Ballyjamesduff, Ulster, Ireland (d. 1900)
Frederick Bridge, English organist and composer, born in Oldbury, Worcestershire (d. 1924)
Edwin Blashfield, American painter who decorated the dome of Library of Congress, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1936)
Thomas Laub, Danish organist and composer, born in Langå, Denmark (d. 1927)
Clinton Hart Merriam, American zoologist and ornithologist, born in New York City (d. 1942)
Armando Diaz, Italian marshal and minister of War (1922-24), born in Naples, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1928)
Paul Painlevé, French Prime Minister (1917, 1925), born in Paris (d. 1933)
Antti Aarne, Finnish folklorist, born in Pori, Finland (d. 1925)
Arnold Sommerfeld, German physicist, born in Königsberg, Prussia (d. 1951)
Ellis Parker Butler, American author (Pigs is Pigs), born in Muscatine, Iowa (d. 1937)
Vítězslav Novák, Czech composer (Slovak Suite; Autumn Symphony), and educator (Prague Conservatory, 1909-20), born in Kamenice nad Lipou, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1949)
Bill Pickett, American rodeo performer (invented bulldogging), born in Jenks-Branch, Texas (d. 1932)
Harry Nelson Pillsbury, American chess player, born in Somerville, Massachusetts (d. 1906)
Arrigo Pedrollo, Italian composer, born in Montebello Vicentino, Veneto, Italy (d. 1964)
Clyde Cessna, American aviator and airplane manufacturer, born in Hawthorne, Louisiana (d. 1954)
Alexander Schmuller, Russian-Dutch violinist and conductor, born in Mazyr, Belarus (d. 1933)
Coretté Alefred [Hardy], American-Russian-Soviet jazz, spiritual and pop lyrical and dramatic soprano, dancer, and actress, born in Churchville, New York (d. 1951)
Evert Cornelis, Dutch conductor and pianist, born in Amsterdam (d. 1931)

Pieter Oud, Dutch politician (Member of Parliament, 1937-33, 1937-38, 1948-63; Mayor of Rotterdam, 1938-41, 1945-52), and co-founder of the Labour Party (PvdA) and the People's Party for Freedom and Democracy (VVD), born in Purmerend, Netherlands (d. 1968)
Rose Wilder Lane, American writer and influential advocate of the American liberation movement, born in De Smet, Dakota Territory (d. 1968)
Áskell Snorrason, Icelandic composer, born in Öndolfsstaðir, Iceland (d. 1970)
David Bomberg, English painter, born in Birmingham, England (d. 1957)
Fritz Lang, Austrian-German-American filmmaker and screenwriter (Metropolis), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1976)
Al Boasberg, American vaudeville, radio and film comedy writer (Jack Benny; Bob Hope; The Marx Brothers), born In Buffalo, New York (d. 1937)
Alexander Rodchenko, Russian painter, photographer, and graphic artist (co-founder of constuctivism), born in St Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1956)[1]
Cyril Ring, American actor (I Wake Up Screaming, The Barber Shop), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1967)
C. R. Swart, South African politician (1st State President of South Africa 1961-67), born in Winburg, Orange Free State (d. 1982)
Philip K Wrigley, American baseball executive (owner Chicago Cubs 1932-77), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1977)
Elbert Frank Cox, African-American mathematician (first African-American to receive PhD in Mathematics), born in Evansville, Indiana (d. 1969)

Carl Ferdinand Cori, Czech-American bio-chemist (Nobel 1947-discovery of how glycogen (animal starch) – a derivative of glucose – is broken down and resynthesized in the body, for use as a store and source of energy), born in Prague (d. 1984)
Wem Lampe, Aruban-Dutch politician (Minister Plenipotentiary of the Dutch Antilles, 1955-67), born in Aruba, Colony of Curaçao and Dependencies (d. 1973)
Nunnally Johnson, American screenwriter and producer, born in Columbus, Georgia (d. 1977)
Grace Moore, American soprano (Metropolitan Opera, 1928-44), and actress (One Night of Love), known as the "Tennessee Nightengale", born in Slabtown, Tennessee (d. 1947)
Josh Malihabadi, Urdu poet of India and Pakistan, born in Malihabad, North-Western Provinces, British India (d. 1982)
Sonny Boy Williamson II [Aleck Miller], American blues harmonica player, singer and songwriter, born in Tallahatchie County, Mississippi (d. 1965) (year of birth disputed)
Hanns Jelinek, Austrian composer, born in Vienna (d. 1969)
Milton H. Erickson, American psychiatrist and psychologist, born in Aurum, Nevada (d. 1980)
Ray Whitley, American country and western singer-songwriter ("Back in the Saddle Again") and actor (Tim Holt films), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1979)
Bruce Drake, American Basketball Hall of Fame coach (University of Oklahoma 1938-55, 200-181), born in The Gentry, Texas (d. 1983)
Gus Mancuso, American baseball catcher (World Series 1931, 33; MLB All Star 1935, 37; NY Giants, St. Louis Cardinals) and broadcaster (Cardinals' radio network), born in Galveston, Texas (d. 1984)
Otto Preminger, Austro-Hungarian-American theatre and film director, producer (Laura,; Advise & Consent; Anatomy of A Murder), and actor (Stalag 17), born in Wiznitz, Austria-Hungary (d. 1986)
Giuseppe Occhialini, Italian physicist (Contribution to the discovery of the pion or pi-meson decay), born in Fossombrone, Pesaro, Italy (d. 1993)
Lin Biao, Chinese Communist Marshal during the Civil War and party leader, born in Huanggang, Hubei, Qing Empire (d. 1971)
Abraham Polonsky, American writer and director (Tell them Willie Boy is Here), born in New York City (d. 1999)
Alfred Manessier, French painter (leaded windows), born in Saint-Ouen, Somme, France (d. 1993)
Władysław Szpilman, Polish pianist and classical composer, born in Sosnowiec, Congress Poland, Russian Empire (d. 2000)
Maisie Fitter, English editor and conservationist, born in Abbey Town, England (d. 1996)
Hans Hellmut Kirst, German author (The Lieutenant Must Be Mad), born in Osterode, Germany (d. 1989)
Lina Bo Bardi, Italian born Brazilian modernist architect (SESC Pompéia Factory), born in Rome, Italy (d. 1992)
Carl Donnell "Kansas" Fields, American jazz drummer, born in Chapman, Kansas (d. 1995)
Margaret Hayes, American actress (Blackboard Jungle, Robert Montgomery Presents), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1977)
Simone Gallimard, French editor and publisher (Mercure de France), born in Étampes, France (d. 1995)
Victor Collin Matthews, British businessman, born in Islington, London (d. 1995)
Alvin "Alvy" Moore, American comic actor (Green Acres, Scream, Intruder), born in Vincennes, Indiana (d. 1997)
George Henderson, Scottish priest (Bishop of Argyll and the Isles from 1977-92), born in Oban, Scotland (d. 1996)
Peter Hansen, American actor (Lee Baldwin in "General Hospital", Mr. Marshall in "War of the Roses"), born in Oakland, California (d. 2017)[1]
Johnny Pate, American jazz bassist (Stuff Smith, James Moody),  pop, soul and R&B arranger and producer (The Impressions, Peabo Bryson), born in Chicago Heights, Illinois
Vladimir Tendryakov, Russian writer (Three, Seven, Ace), born in Makarovskaya, Vologda Oblast (d. 1984)
(Georgios) "George" Savalas, Greek-American actor (Kojak; Kelly's Heroes), born in The Bronx, New York (d. 1985)
Beverly Hanson, Americangolfer(Western Open	1956; Titleholders C'ship 1958; Women's PGA C'ship 1955), born in Fargo, North Dakota (d. 2014)
Glen "Frosty" Little, American circus clown (Ringling Brothers, 1968-91), and instructor (Ringling Bros. and Barnum & Bailey Clown College, 1980-87), born in Genoa, Nebraska (d. 2010)
Brian Tobin, Australian tennis executive (President International Tennis Federation 1991-99; International Tennis HOF), born in Perth, Australia (d. 2024)
Charles Reinhart, American proponent of modern dance and director of the American Dance Festival (1968-2011), born in Summit, New Jersey (d. 2025)
Fazu Aliyeva, Russian poet, novelist and journalist who helped develop Dagestani in Russian literature, born in Khunzakhsky District, Soviet Union (d. 2016)
James Cleveland, American Grammy Award-winning gospel singer-songwriter, pianist, arranger ("Jesus Is the Best Thing That Ever Happened to Me), and minister, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1991)
Sheldon Lee Glashow, American theoretical physicist (Electroweak theory, Georgi–Glashow model), born in New York City
Bernard van Beurden, Dutch composer and music educator, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2016)
Edward Daly, Irish Bishop "Bloody Sunday" priest, born in Belleek, County Fermanagh (d. 2016)
Art Davis, American bassist ("A Time Remembered"), born in Hamburg, Pennsylvania (d. 2007)
Eberhard Jüngel, German Lutherantheologian, born in Magdeburg, Germany (d. 2021)
Joan Didion, American author and journalist (The Year of Magical Thinking; Slouching Toward Bethlehem), born in Sacramento, California (d. 2021)
Calvin Trillin, American author (New Yorker), born in Kansas City, Missouri
Marise Chamberlain, New Zealand athlete (Women's mile WR 1962–67 2:01.4; Olympic bronze 800m 1964), born in Christchurch, New Zealand (d. 2024)
Yury Vlasov, Ukrainian weightlifter (Olympic gold USSR heavyweight 1960, 64; World C'ship gold x 4) and writer, born in Makeyevka, Ukraine (d. 2021)
James Lee Burke, American writer (Dave Robicheaux series), born in Houston, Texas
Robert Freeman, English photo-journalist and graphic designer (early Beatles album art and film posters), born in London (d. 2019)[1]
Gennady Sapunov, Russian Greco-Roman wrestler (World C'ship gold lightweight 1963, 65; silver 1966) and coach (Soviet GR wrestling team 1979-90), born in Ust-Muya, Russia (d. 2023)
J. D. McDuffie, American auto racer (653 x NASCAR Cup Series races; 106 x top-10 finishes), born in Upper Little River Township, North Carolina (d. 1991)
